Sample Outputs: zai-org/GLM-5.1

− Iteration 1 — Avg Score: 81.8
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 75.9
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's air filtration system hummed with a persistent mechanical drone. Arthur Neegan stared at the riveted steel bulkhead, tracing the seam lines with his eyes as though they might offer a map out of his current predicament. Beside him, occupying the fold-out crash couch with a rigid, mechanical stillness, sat his escort. The man hadn't spoken a word since they had boarded the pinnace at Olympus Mons. He simply sat there in his charcoal-grey uniform, the silver starburst of the Antares Diplomatic Corp catching the dim cabin lighting with every subtle vibration of the thrusters. Diplomatic Corp. Arthur tasted the phrase in his mind, finding it bitter. In the sprawling bureaucracy of the Solar System, a diplomat was merely a soldier who killed you with paperwork instead of a pulse-rifle. The Antares Corp was the sharpest point of that particular spear, enforcing the interests of the outer colonies and their syndicates with a velvet grip. Arthur shifted, the coarse fabric of his standard-issue miner’s coveralls rustling against the synthetic upholstery. "You still haven't told me where we're going," he said, his voice cutting cleanly through the drone of the scrubbers. The escort didn't turn his head. "To a meeting, Mister Neegan." "With whom?" "Someone who finds your recent geological surveys... compelling." Arthur leaned back, folding his arms. He had lived on Mars long enough to know the math of the system. The corporations held the leases on the land, and the laborers held the leases on their lives. As a first-generation settler, Arthur had been born into that precise, mathematical misery. He had clawed his way into the engineering corps, trading the pickaxe for a seismograph, earning a modest, unassuming plot of regolith in the process. And then, three days ago, his ground-penetrating radar had hit a resonance spike that didn't quit. An Azra vein. A vein so vast it redefined the word. Azra was the universe’s impossible element. It didn't just undergo radioactive decay; it accelerated it, collapsing the half-life of surrounding isotopes into nanoseconds. In a reactor, a gram of Azra could power a starship to Alpha Centauri. In a munition, a sliver could reduce a city to glass. It was the fulcrum upon which the modern age balanced, and Arthur had just stumbled upon the lever. The pinnace decelerated with a low groan, and the viewport’s polarized glass shifted from black to a star-speckled void. They were docking with a cruiser. When the airlock hissed open, Arthur was escorted down a corridor of brushed aluminum and sterile polymer. The retrofuturistic aesthetic was immaculate—everything was functional, geometric, stripped of ornamentation, yet humming with an underlying power that spoke of advanced physics. They stopped before a set of double doors bearing the Antares starburst, and his escort gestured him inside. The room was a study in controlled intimidation. A long, polished mahogany table dominated the space—an ostentatious display of dead Earth wood, meant to impress upon any visitor the sheer wealth of the Antares syndicate. Behind it stood a man whose silver hair was cropped close to his skull, his features sharp and unsmiling. "Arthur Neegan," the man said. It was not a question. "I am Councilor Vane. Please, sit." Arthur remained standing. "I’d rather know why I was brought here at the point of a blaster, Councilor. Antares diplomacy seems a lot like common kidnapping." Vane’s lips twitched, a ghost of a smile that never reached his eyes. "A misunderstanding of scale, Neegan. You are a man of science; you understand that local anomalies must sometimes be constrained for the good of the broader system." Vane folded his hands on the table. "You have found Azra. A great deal of it." "I filed the survey with the Mining Bureau," Arthur said evenly. "It’s my claim." "The Bureau is a slow, inefficient machine. By the time their clerks process your claim, every corporate cartel from here to the Jovian moons will have dispatched a fleet to carve up your plot, and you with it. We are here to offer you... structure. Protection." "At what price?" Vane leaned forward, the starlight from the viewport catching the hard angles of his face. "The Earth coalition is dying, Neegan. Their reactors are failing, their fleets are rusting. The outer systems are the future. If your vein is controlled by the inner planets, it will be forged into warheads to subjugate the frontier. If it is controlled by Antares, it will fuel the colonization ships that will carry humanity to the stars. We are offering you the chance to lease your claim directly to the Antares Diplomatic Corp. You will receive a one-percent royalty, paid in guaranteed corporate scrip, and you will live out a very comfortable, very quiet life." Arthur felt the cold logic of it pressing in on him. A one-percent royalty on an infinite vein was still a fortune. It was a mathematician's paradise—more wealth than a miner could ever dream of. But it was the mathematics of a cage. "And if I refuse?" Vane’s expression didn't change, but the temperature in the room seemed to drop. "Then the political realities of the situation will assert themselves. You are a single man on a patch of dirt, Arthur. The system requires Azra. If you do not align with us, you will find yourself aligned against us, and the universe is a very unforgiving place for those who stand alone in the path of progress." The threat was polite, almost conversational, which made it all the more chilling. Arthur looked at the polished mahogany, seeing in its dead wood the fate of every laborer who had ever signed a corporate lease. He had uncovered the greatest discovery in human history, and in doing so, had reduced himself to a variable in an equation he no longer controlled. The hum of the air scrubbers seemed to grow louder in the silence.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a solid grasp of Asimovian science fiction conventions and successfully delivers on most of the prompt requirements. The writing captures the classic sci-fi aesthetic with its focus on political machinations, corporate power structures, and the intersection of individual agency with larger systemic forces. The worldbuilding is effectively executed, establishing the retrofuturistic setting through concrete details like "riveted steel bulkheads," "brushed aluminum corridors," and the ostentatious mahogany table that serves as a symbol of corporate wealth. The concept of Azra as a reality-altering element is well-integrated into the narrative's political framework. The dialogue between Arthur and Councilor Vane carries the scene effectively, with Vane's measured threats and Arthur's cautious resistance feeling authentic to their respective positions. The power dynamic is clearly established through both verbal exchanges and environmental details. Vane's characterization as a polite but ruthless corporate operative comes through in his calculated speech patterns. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its setup and execution. The scenario follows familiar beats of the lone individual versus corporate power, and while competently handled, it doesn't offer particularly fresh insights into this dynamic. Arthur's characterization, while believable, remains somewhat surface-level - we understand his situation but don't deeply feel his internal conflict. The prose is generally clean and functional, though it occasionally veers toward overwrought territory with phrases like "the universe's impossible element" and "the fulcrum upon which the modern age balanced." The pacing is appropriate, building tension through the journey and culminating in the confrontation. The ending effectively establishes the stakes and Arthur's predicament without resolving anything prematurely. The final image of Arthur seeing his fate reflected in the "dead wood" of the table provides a fitting metaphor for his situation, though it borders on heavy-handed symbolism. The piece succeeds in establishing the larger political intrigue and hints at the broader conflict between Earth coalition and outer colonies, fulfilling the prompt's requirement for big-picture worldbuilding. The technical aspects of the writing are competent, with smooth sentence flow and consistent tone throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The red safelight turns everything into a confession. Elliot's hands, steady with the tongs. His jaw, sharp but always softening when he concentrates. The way his chipped black nail polish catches the light as he slides the developer tray toward me. "Eighteen seconds," I say, because someone has to keep count, and I'm good at keeping count. I'm good at keeping a lot of things—schedules, boundaries, the upper hand. But I'm losing count now. Of the nights we've been in here. Of the reasons I told myself this was just about the project. "Seventeen, sixteen—" Elliot's voice is barely a whisper. He's always been quiet, but in the darkroom, his silence has a different texture. Intentional. Like he's saving his words for something worth saying. I watch the image emerge in the chemical bath. A self-portrait he'd shot last week—his face half-obscured by a sheer black curtain, one eye visible, rimmed with smudged eyeliner. Beautiful. Uncertain. The kind of photo that makes my chest ache in a way I don't know what to do with. I usually know what to do with everything. "Elliot." I keep my voice even, controlled. "This is incredible." He exhales like he's been holding his breath for the entire semester. "You think?" "I don't say things I don't mean." I pull the print with the tongs, drop it in the stop bath. "You know that." He does know that. He's watched me critique other students with surgical precision, watched me dismantle weak concepts and rebuild them stronger. He's watched me be Dani—the Dani who takes up space, who speaks first, who never apologizes for wanting. But I haven't told him what I want. Not in words. "Dani." He says my name like a question, and something in his tone makes me look up. His fingers are gripping the edge of the counter, knuckles white beneath the black polish. "Can I—there's something I need to say. And I need to just say it, okay? Before I overthink it." My instinct is to take over, to guide him, to make it easier. But I bite my tongue and nod. "I've been questioning things." He swallows. "Gender things. Not just— not just the clothes, not just the makeup. I don't know what I am. I just know that 'boy' doesn't... it doesn't fit all the way anymore. Some days it doesn't fit at all." The red light makes his eyes look almost amber. He's trembling, and I want to reach out, want to pull him close, want to tell him exactly what he needs to hear. That's what I do. I fix things. I take charge. But this isn't mine to fix. "Thank you for telling me." My voice comes out softer than I intended. "That couldn't have been easy." "It wasn't." A fragile laugh. "But you're... you're the only person I've said it out loud to. I don't know why. You're just—" "Assertive?" I offer, and I'm rewarded with a real smile. "Intimidating as hell. But also... safe. You're safe, Dani." Safe. The word lands somewhere between my ribs. I've been called a lot of things—aggressive, intense, too much—but never safe. And never with this kind of reverence. I don't know what to do with the way my pulse is racing. I'm always attracted to certainty, to people who know exactly who they are, who match my energy with equal force. I like defined edges, clear lines. And Elliot is all blur and becoming, all questions and maybes. So why do I want to kiss him so badly my teeth hurt? "The project," I say, because I need a moment. "We should focus on the project." "Right. Yeah." He turns back to the enlarger, and I watch him fumble with the height adjustment. "I was thinking maybe we could do something about—about masks. About the versions of ourselves we perform versus—" "Versus who we are in the dark," I finish, and he looks at me with something like wonder. "Yeah. Exactly." We work in silence for a while. I measure the chemicals. He adjusts the timer. Our movements have become choreographed over these late nights, a dance I hadn't realized we were learning. But then his elbow catches the stack of photo paper I'd left by the easel, and it slides, and I lunge forward, and he reaches for it at the same time, and— The safelight floods the paper. Exposed. Ruined. "Shit." I stare at the stack, now completely useless. "Shit, shit, shit." "I'm sorry—I didn't mean—" Elliot's voice is shaking again, and I realize he thinks I'm angry. He thinks I'm going to snap at him, take over, assert control. Instead, I start laughing. "Dani?" "We just destroyed twenty dollars of paper." I'm still laughing, helpless, undone. "And we're going to have to explain to Professor Chen that we wasted an entire stack because we were too busy having feelings to pay attention." He stares at me for a beat—then laughs too, startled and bright. The sound fills the darkroom, bounces off the chemical-stained walls, and I think: there it is. That's what I want. That sound. "Dani." He says my name again, and this time it's not a question. It's an answer. I look at him. At his smudged eyeliner and his thrift-store lace shirt and his uncertain, beautiful face. At the person he's becoming, right here in the red light. "Can I—" I start, then stop. My hand hovers near his, not quite touching. "I don't want to take over. I don't want to assume. But I really want to hold your hand, and I'm out of practice at asking instead of just... doing." He bridges the gap. His fingers are cold from the chemicals, and they fit between mine like a question neither of us needs to answer yet. "I don't know what this means," he says quietly. "For either of us." "Me neither." And for once, the uncertainty doesn't terrify me. "But maybe that's okay. Maybe we can figure it out." Outside, the campus is dark. In here, we're bathed in red, developing slowly, waiting to see what emerges.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing vulnerability and growth. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, with the red light creating an intimate, confessional atmosphere that enhances the emotional weight of Elliot's revelation. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her natural tendency to take charge and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Her attraction to certainty being challenged by her feelings for someone in a state of becoming creates compelling internal tension. Elliot's vulnerability and tentative self-discovery are handled with sensitivity and believability. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery ("The red safelight turns everything into a confession") and natural dialogue that reveals character. The pacing builds effectively toward the emotional climax of Elliot's confession and their tentative connection. The photography metaphors are well-integrated without being heavy-handed. The piece successfully avoids several potential pitfalls: it doesn't rush toward resolution, doesn't oversimplify complex identity questions, and maintains appropriate uncertainty about the future. The ending feels earned rather than artificially positive, acknowledging that both characters are in "uncharted territory." Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal monologue could be more subtle, and the destroyed photo paper incident, while serving the plot, feels slightly convenient. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise well-crafted piece that handles sensitive subject matter with nuance and respect. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 80.9
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma The screech of brakes cut through the morning air like a knife through gelato. Ben looked up from his Lonely Planet guide just in time to see death approaching on two wheels—a red Vespa piloted by a woman who seemed personally offended by his existence. He stumbled backward, his shoulder connecting with an American tourist's selfie stick, his guidebook pinwheeling into the fountain's spray. "*Cazzo!*" The rider yanked off her helmet, unleashing a cascade of dark hair and a torrent of Italian that Ben suspected was not complimentary. "*Che fai, stronzo?* You stand in the middle of the street reading a book? In Roma?" "I'm—I'm terribly sorry, I was just—the fountain—your driving was rather—" Ben gestured helplessly at the cobblestones, his face crimson. "*Mi dispiace?*" She stared at him. Then, unexpectedly, laughed—a sound like church bells and broken glass. "Your accent. It's like a cat dying in a library." "That's... quite specific." "Alessandra." She extended her hand, rings glinting. "And you are?" "Ben. Benjamin, actually. Dr. Benjamin Shaw, though that sounds rather pompous now I've said it aloud." "Dot-tor." She rolled the word around her mouth like a hard candy. "And what does Dot-tor Benjamin Shaw do in Roma, besides nearly dying in front of the Trevi?" "Renaissance architectural history. On sabbatical. From Cambridge." "Ah." Her eyes sparkled with mischief. "So you know things about old buildings but nothing about looking both ways." --- Her Vespa had a dent on the left side—a deep, colorful scrape showing primer beneath the red paint, like a war wound she wore proudly. Ben noticed it when she gestured imperiously for him to climb on. "I'm not getting on that." "Then walk, Dot-tor. I'm showing you Roma. The real one, not the one in your wet book." "I don't even know you." "And you won't know Roma from a book. Move your ass—scusa, your *dignified British ass*—onto this seat." He climbed on. --- The coffee bar in Trastevere was the size of a closet and twice as crowded. Alessandra shouldered her way to the counter, ordering with rapid-fire hand gestures that seemed to communicate volumes. When the espressos arrived, she downed hers in one shot. Ben sipped his carefully. "This is... actually extraordinary." "Of course it is. Now—your wallet." "I'm sorry?" She was already holding it. His brown leather wallet, which had definitely been in his jacket pocket. She waved it at him, grinning. "Too easy, Dot-tor. In Roma, you pay attention or you pay everything." "How did you—" "Next time, closer to body." She slipped it back into his hands, her fingers brushing his, lingering one heartbeat too long. "I'm teaching you survival." Her own fingers, he noticed, had a silver lighter tucked between them that hadn't been there before. From the counter. He almost said something. He didn't. --- The Crypt of the Capuchins was not in any itinerary Ben had planned. Alessandra dragged him past the sign—thousands of bones arranged in baroque patterns, skulls and femurs creating grim chandeliers. "This is macabre," he whispered. "This is history, Dot-tor. Your thing, no?" She leaned close, her voice dropping. "We all become decoration eventually. Better to enjoy the arrangement now." Her hand found his in the dim light. She didn't let go. Neither did he. --- The afternoon dissolved into chaos. A market where Alessandra haggled so aggressively for sunglasses that the vendor surrendered just to be rid of her. A hidden courtyard where she produced a bottle of wine from nowhere—"A magician never reveals her secrets"—and they drank from the bottle, passing it back and forth, her lipstick marking the glass. At the Pantheon, she made him close his eyes and feel the rain falling through the oculus. "Rain in a church," she murmured. "Even God breaks the rules sometimes." When he opened his eyes, a small marble cherub from a nearby stall had materialized in her palm. She pressed it into his hand. "For your desk at Cambridge. So you remember that Roma is not a book." "I couldn't possibly—" "You already did." She smiled, enigmatic. "Keep it." --- Sunset found them on the Ponte Sant'Angelo, the Tiber burning gold below. Ben was sunburned, disheveled, his shirt untucked, his guidebook abandoned in some café hours ago. He looked like a man who had been thoroughly unmade. Alessandra straddled her Vespa, helmet dangling from her fingers. "This was—" Ben started. "Insane? Illegal? Very, very stupid?" "I was going to say extraordinary." "Same thing." She started the engine. The scratch on the Vespa's side caught the dying light. "So, Dot-tor. Tomorrow?" "Tomorrow?" "I pick you up at nine. Don't be late. Don't bring the book." She was already pulling away when he called after her: "I don't even know where you live!" She called back over her shoulder, laughing: "No, Dot-tor. But you know where to find me." Ben stood on the bridge, the stolen cherub warm in his pocket, and realized he was smiling like a man who had lost something essential and found something dangerous in its place. He couldn't wait for tomorrow.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue and action rather than exposition. Alessandra's fiery, mischievous nature contrasts effectively with Ben's polite, academic demeanor, and their chemistry develops naturally throughout their day together. The narrative structure works well, using short vignette-style scenes that create momentum and mirror the whirlwind nature of their encounter. The dialogue feels authentic and reveals character - Alessandra's mix of Italian and English, her directness, and Ben's flustered politeness all ring true. The author handles the cultural clash with nuance, avoiding stereotypes while still highlighting genuine differences. The incorporation of Alessandra's kleptomania is particularly well-executed - it's shown rather than told, woven naturally into scenes (the wallet, the lighter, the cherub) without heavy-handed explanation. This adds layers to her character and creates an undercurrent of danger and unpredictability. The Roman setting is vividly rendered through specific locations and sensory details. The author demonstrates good knowledge of the city and uses it as more than mere backdrop - the locations reflect the characters' journey and emotional states. However, there are some weaknesses. The transformation of Ben from cautious academic to willing participant in Alessandra's adventures happens quite rapidly, though this is somewhat mitigated by the compressed timeframe. Some metaphors occasionally veer toward the overwrought ("church bells and broken glass," "cat dying in a library"), though they generally work within the romantic genre conventions. The ending strikes an appropriate note - romantic and hopeful without being saccharine, leaving questions about their future while providing emotional satisfaction. The piece successfully establishes the foundation for a larger story while functioning as a complete narrative arc. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I'm wiping down the sticky ring left by a Borvian freighter captain when the door seal hisses open. The Last Outpost rarely sees new faces. The asteroid belt is a labyrinth of rock and ice, far from the central shipping lanes, and folks who end up here usually mean to. The stranger who steps through the threshold looks like they definitely didn't mean to. Their tentacled dreads are askew, tangled with rock dust and what looks suspiciously like crystallized shuttle coolant. Their eyes—three of them, a deep, startled violet—are darting around the dimly lit bar like they’re tracking targeting lasers. They navigate past a cluster of loud mercury-miners and quietly plop down at my bar. "Vega whisky, neat." Their voice is a low, breathless rasp. They pause, swallowing hard, the gills on their neck fluttering rapidly. "Better make that a double." "Coming right up," I say, keeping my voice low and steady. When you're running from something, sudden movements are a language you fluently speak. I reach for the middle shelf—the good stuff, not the paint-thinner Jax prefers for his benders. I pour two fingers, then add a third for good measure, and slide the tumbler across the recycled polymer counter. "Rough flight?" "You could say that." They wrap their long, suction-cupped fingers around the glass, downing half of it in one desperate swallow. A shudder runs through their slender frame, rippling up to their dreads. "You could also say I'm a colossal idiot. Both would be accurate." "Well," I say, leaning against the counter, offering a soft, easy smile. "Idiots are my favorite kind of customer. They tip better than geniuses. I'm Nets." "Kael." They don't offer a hand, which is fine. Handshakes are overrated, and Kael’s hands are busy clutching that whisky like it’s a life preserver. "Welcome to The Last Outpost, Kael. It's mostly quiet, mostly dry, and the only rule is no shooting holes in my hull. The repair bills come out of your tab." A weak chuckle escapes them. "No shooting. Sounds peaceful." "Mm, mostly." I glance toward the back corner, where Jax and his mercury-miners are getting rowdy over a hand of cards. "The peace comes and goes. Depends on the shift." Kael takes another sip, slower this time, letting the amber liquid burn away whatever chill they brought in from the black. "How far out are we? From the nearest Vanguard checkpoint, I mean." I pause my wiping. The Vanguard. The militarized policing force of the Andromeda inner rim. Nobody asks about the Vanguard unless they’re trying to avoid them, or they *are* them. And Kael definitely isn't Vanguard. "Depends on who's asking," I say carefully. "But for you? About three standard jumps. Why?" Kael's violet eyes snap to mine, suddenly sharp, suddenly terrified. "Because they're coming. Maybe two hours behind me, maybe less. I slipped through the Carina nebula, but they tagged my drive signature." My stomach drops, but I keep my face perfectly neutral. It's a talent I cultivated in my past life. "The Vanguard doesn't chase asteroid-hoppers for a drive violation, Kael. What did you take?" "I didn't *take* anything," Kael hisses, their dreads tightening defensively around their shoulders. "I *translated*. It was a dead language, Nets! A dead dialect from a collapsed colony in the Vega system. It was just data, historical records, poetry! But the Vanguard classified it as a weaponized algorithm. They said it's a cognitive hazard." I stare at them. "Poetry?" "That's what I said!" Kael groans, draining the rest of the double. "But the Vanguard doesn't care. They wiped the entire archive on Vega-9 to contain it. I managed to download a fragment before the purge. I thought—I thought it belonged in a museum, or a university, not locked in some black-site server." "And now you've brought a cognitive hazard to my cozy little bar," I say, my voice dangerously flat. "It's not a hazard! It's just words!" "Words that make people do what, exactly? Spontaneously combust? Join the infantry?" Kael hesitates, their gills fluttering again. "I... don't know, exactly. I haven't fully translated it yet." "Perfect." I resist the urge to bang my head against the taps. "So you're running from a Vanguard kill-squad over a language you can't even read." "I'm a linguist, not a revolutionary," Kael says miserably. "I just didn't want it erased." Across the room, the air shifts. Jax slams his thick, metallic hand down on the card table, his ruddy face twisting into a scowl. "You cheating son of a slag!" Jax roars, shoving his chair back. "Sit down, Jax," I call out, my voice carrying over the low hum of the atmospheric scrubbers. "You're just mad you drew a dead man's hand." "I'm mad because this scab is stacking the deck!" Jax steps forward, his massive frame tensing. The smaller merchant at the table panics. He stands abruptly, his elbow catching the edge of his tray. The motion is sharp, clumsy. A heavy pint glass goes tumbling. *Crash.* The sound shatters the low murmur of the bar. Glass sprays across the grated floor, glinting under the amber neon. The reaction is instantaneous. Kael violently flinches, their dreads whipping backward as they spin around on their stool, hands raised in a defensive posture. Their breath hitches into a sharp, ragged gasp. For a terrifying second, their three eyes are wide and unseeing, trapped in whatever nightmare they just escaped out there in the black. I vault the counter in two seconds flat. I step right into Kael's line of sight, blocking their view of the spilled glass and the looming miner. "Hey," I say, snapping my fingers softly. "Kael. Look at me. Just me. It's just a glass. Jax is being an ass. We're fine." Kael blinks, their violet eyes slowly refocusing on my face. Their chest heaves, but the immediate panic recedes. "Sorry. I'm... sorry." "Don't apologize to me." I glance over my shoulder. "Jax! Clean up your mess, you overgrown drill-bit! And you owe me for the glass!" Jax grumbles something foul under his breath, but he grabs a rag, knowing better than to push his luck when I use that tone. I turn back to Kael, keeping my voice low. "Listen to me. The Vanguard doesn't come out this far. This is the outer rim. We're in the debris belt. If they want to find this station, they have to navigate a shifting labyrinth of iron and ice. We have time." Kael shakes their head, their dreads still trembling. "You don't understand. They sent a Harrier." My blood runs cold. A Harrier. The Vanguard’s deep-space interceptors. Fast, silent, and heavily armed. Not a patrol boat. A hunter. "Are you sure?" I ask, my voice barely a whisper. "I saw the drive signature on the long-range scanner before I jumped," Kael says, their voice hollow. "They tracked me through the nebula. They know I'm here." I look around The Last Outpost. Jax is grumbling as he wipes up beer and glass. Old Mira is dozing in her usual booth. The merchants are dealing cards again. A lazy hub. My home. The only place in the galaxy that tolerated my flaws, my past, my quiet desperation to just be nobody's savior. I can't let a Harrier tear this place apart. But I can't throw Kael to the wolves, either. Not over some dead poetry. "Okay," I say quietly, my mind racing. "Okay. We need to think. Does anyone else know you came here?" "No. I bounced my signal off a dozen dead relays. But the Harrier will—" The main console on the wall chimes. A sharp, piercing tone that cuts through the jukebox and the chatter. The sound of an incoming ship requesting docking clearance. My heart hammers against my ribs. We don't get unannounced visitors. I look at Kael. Their violet eyes are wide with terror. The console chimes again.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi story set in The Last Outpost with Nets as the bartender protagonist. The author successfully establishes conflict through Kael's arrival with stolen linguistic data pursued by Vanguard forces, and wisely leaves this conflict unresolved as requested. The dialogue percentage appears to meet the 60% target, and the first-person present tense is maintained consistently throughout. The characterization is particularly strong. Nets emerges as a compelling protagonist with hints of a mysterious past, displaying competence in handling both bar situations and crisis management. Her voice feels authentic - practical, observant, and carrying the weight of someone who has fled their own troubles. Kael is well-developed as a sympathetic figure - a linguist caught up in events beyond their understanding, with believable physical and emotional responses to trauma. The world-building is efficiently executed without overwhelming exposition. Details like "crystallized shuttle coolant," "mercury-miners," and the Vanguard's classification system create a lived-in universe. The author demonstrates skill in balancing sci-fi elements with the cozy atmosphere - the bar feels genuinely welcoming despite the underlying tension. The prose quality is generally high, with natural sentence flow and effective imagery. Descriptions like "tentacled dreads askew, tangled with rock dust" and "gills on their neck fluttering rapidly" create vivid alien characteristics without feeling forced. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining atmospheric detail. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - character development, world-building, and plot advancement. Nets' bartender persona comes through clearly in her interactions, while Kael's desperation and confusion feel genuine. The escalating tension is well-managed, particularly in the scene where broken glass triggers Kael's trauma response. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the mysterious stranger with dangerous knowledge being pursued by authorities is a familiar trope, though it's executed competently here. The pacing occasionally slows during exposition, though not severely. The ending builds appropriate tension with the incoming ship, creating a strong hook for future chapters. The emotional complexity is noteworthy - Nets' internal conflict between self-preservation and helping others, Kael's guilt over their academic curiosity causing danger, and the broader theme of preserving knowledge versus safety create meaningful depth beyond the surface plot. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South ## Chapter Two The saxophone finds me before I find anything else. It's coming from somewhere ahead, weaving through the humidity like it owns this air, like it was born in it. I follow the sound because what else am I going to do? I'm standing on Canal Street with a backpack that has three days of clothes, forty-seven dollars cash, a dead phone, and the phantom weight of a door slamming behind me. Mom's face does this thing in my memory—hardens, closes, like a window being nailed shut. *We don't know what you're talking about, Taylor. This is just more of your drama.* The crowd moves around me like I'm a rock in a river. Nobody looks at me. In White Pine, everybody looked at me. Everybody knew the Millers' weird kid, the one who set off fireworks in the locker room freshman year, who the guidance counselor called "attention-seeking" in that careful voice people use when they've already decided you're a problem. I start walking. The saxophone gets louder. Bourbon Street hits me like a fever dream. Neon bleeding into the wet pavement, a woman in a sequined dress laughing too loud, someone handing out beads I don't want. The smell of fried food and garbage and something sweet—beignets, maybe, from that place I saw on a travel show once. I've never been anywhere. Never left Tennessee. Now I'm standing in the middle of a city that doesn't sleep, that probably doesn't even blink, and I should be terrified. I should be calling home. I'm not. I'm not doing that. The saxophone player is old, Black, wearing a suit that's seen better decades. His case lies open on the ground, scattered with bills and coins. His eyes are closed. The music pours out of him like he's just the instrument, like something bigger is playing *him*. I stop. I listen. Something cracks open in my chest—grief or relief or some mutant combination of both. "That's 'My Favorite Things,'" someone says beside me. "Coltrane version. Not the Sound of Music shit." I turn. The voice belongs to a girl—maybe seventeen, maybe my age, maybe ageless. She's leaning against a lamppost like it's holding her up. Shaved head with a patch of purple growing in at the roots. A scar through one eyebrow. She's wearing a tank top despite the evening chill, and her arms are covered in pen drawings—flowers, snakes, words I can't read in this light. "You know Coltrane?" I ask, because it's the first thing that comes out, and immediately I hate myself for how small my voice sounds. "Everyone knows Coltrane." She looks at me properly. "Everyone who's been anywhere knows Coltrane. You from somewhere?" "Does it matter?" "It tells me whether you're running *to* something or *from* something." She pushes off the lamppost. "I'm Maren." "Taylor." "Cool name." She says it like she means it. Like it's not a question or a punchline. The saxophone player shifts into something slower, sadder. Maren watches him, and I watch her face, and there's something there—some recognition. Like we're both standing at the edge of the same cliff, looking down. "You got somewhere to stay tonight?" she asks. "I'm figuring it out." "That's a no." She laughs, but not mean. "I know a spot. Not a shelter—those places ask questions. This is just... a place. Safe enough." I should say no. I should. The counselor's voice echoes: *concerning behavioral patterns, impulsive decision-making, failure to consider consequences.* But the counselor never had to sit across from a dinner table where everyone pretended the last twenty minutes didn't happen. The counselor never felt their own mother look at them like they'd been replaced by a stranger. "Show me," I say. Maren grins. "You got any money?" "Some." "Good. You're buying beignets first. Rule of the city—you don't walk around here on an empty stomach. That's how you end up crying on a curb before midnight." "Is that what you did?" Her grin flickers. "Something like that." We walk. The saxophone fades behind us, but I can still feel it somewhere in my ribs, vibrating. Maren moves through the crowd like water—confident, easy. Like she belongs. I try to copy her, but I keep flinching at noises, checking over my shoulder. "First time away from home?" she asks, not looking at me. "Is it that obvious?" "You've got that look. The one that says you're waiting for someone to tell you you're allowed to be here." She glances back. "Nobody's gonna give you permission, Taylor. You gotta take it." We reach a café that glows yellow in the dark. The line is long but Maren walks past it, waves at the guy behind the counter, and suddenly we're sitting at a tiny table with a plate of powdered sugar bombs that might be the best thing I've ever eaten. My throat tightens. Mom used to make pancakes on Sunday mornings. Before. Before everything got complicated. "Hey," Maren says softly. "Wherever you just went—come back." I didn't realize I'd left. I swallow sugar and almost-crying and look at her. Really look. The pen drawings on her arms. The scar. The way she's watching me like she's cataloging my exits. "You're a runaway too," I say. "Was. Now I'm just... here." She licks powdered sugar off her thumb. "The difference between running away and starting over is just time, I think. And whether you let yourself stop running." I want to ask what she's running from. I want to ask about the scar, the drawings, the way she said *safe enough* instead of *safe*. But I don't. Some questions you earn. "Thanks for the beignets," I say instead. "Thanks for not asking me a bunch of bullshit questions." "Yet." "Yet." She almost smiles. "Come on. I'll show you the spot. Tomorrow, we figure out the rest." *The rest.* What does that even mean? I don't have a plan. I have forty-seven dollars and a dead phone and a name that finally feels like mine. I have a city that doesn't know me, a stranger who might be a friend, and a saxophone still playing somewhere in the dark. It's not much. But it's more than I had yesterday. I follow her into the night.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The author successfully captures Taylor's voice through a blend of vulnerability and defensive bravado that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The internal monologue effectively weaves between present observations and painful memories, creating emotional depth without becoming overwrought. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a complex protagonist - impulsive yet thoughtful, hurt yet resilient. Maren is well-crafted as a fellow runaway who serves as both potential ally and mystery, with her own layers suggested through physical details and careful dialogue. The dynamic between them feels natural and earned. The New Orleans setting is vividly rendered through sensory details - the saxophone music, humid air, neon lights, and beignets create an immersive atmosphere. The city becomes almost a character itself, offering both promise and uncertainty. The prose strikes an effective balance between lyrical moments and grounded realism. Dialogue feels authentic to the characters' ages and circumstances, with Maren's streetwise confidence contrasting nicely with Taylor's uncertainty. The conversation flows naturally and reveals character while advancing the plot. The pacing works well, moving from disorientation to tentative connection without rushing the emotional beats. The ending strikes an appropriate note - cautiously hopeful but not unrealistically positive given Taylor's circumstances. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly overwrought, and a few instances where emotional beats could be more subtly handled. The piece also relies somewhat on familiar runaway narrative elements, though it executes them well. Overall, this is accomplished young adult fiction that treats its subject matter with appropriate gravity while maintaining readability and emotional engagement. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 78.2
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The morning sun crept over the ruins of what had once been a shopping mall, casting long shadows across Midas's makeshift shelter—a repurposed freezer unit that no longer froze anything except, occasionally, Midas's enthusiasm for living. "The way I see it," said Midas, addressing his companion, "we've got two options. Option one: we stay here and definitely starve. Option two: we go out there and probably die." Chewy stared at him with those unblinking glass eyes that had seen the Before Time, the golden-brown irises catching the light in a way that suggested ancient wisdom, or possibly just a manufacturing quirk in a factory that had long since crumbled to dust. "That's not a vote," Midas clarified. "You don't get a vote. You're an animatronic koala." Chewy's mechanism clicked—the one that, in better days, had made his little mouth move in sync with pre-recorded fun facts about eucalyptus. Now it just made him sound like he was chewing. Hence the name. "Fine. You get a vote. But if you're voting for option one, you're an idiot." Another click. Midas chose to interpret this as enthusiastic support for option two. They'd found the graffiti three days ago, scrawled across the side of a collapsed overpass in faded green spray paint: *GREENVALE LIVES. 3 NORTH, 2 WEST. FOLLOW THE TEETH.* "The teeth," Midas muttered for the hundredth time, strapping Chewy to his pack. "Who writes 'follow the teeth'? What teeth? Whose teeth?" *Click-click*, said Chewy. "Don't get philosophical on me." Three north and two west meant navigating through the Barrows—a stretch of suburban decay where the houses had grown into each other like fungal colonies, creating a labyrinth of collapsed drywall and reclaimed vegetation. It was also, as Midas recalled from his last visit, home to a pack of feral dogs the size of ponies and the temperament of a hangover. But Greenvale, if it existed, meant people. Supplies. Maybe even working plumbing, though Midas had long ago lowered his standards to "water that didn't glow." They set off at dawn, which Midas had learned was the optimal time to avoid both the dogs (nocturnal) and the roving bands of scavengers who operated on a philosophy of "finders keepers, survivors weepers." For the first hour, things went surprisingly well. Midas attributed this to Chewy's presence. "Don't look so smug," he told the koala. "We haven't done anything yet." The trouble began at the second mile north, where the road narrowed between two collapsed townhouses. Midas spotted the first tooth—a massive molar, human-sized but definitely not human, wedged into a fence post like a warning. "Follow the teeth," he said. "Right. Great. Whose brilliant idea was this?" *Click*, Chewy replied, somehow managing to convey that it had, in fact, been Midas's idea. The second tooth appeared fifty yards later. Then a third. They led down into a basement stairway that smelled like rust and regret. "Absolutely not," said Midas. *Click-click-click.* "Since when do you have opinions about basements?" The sound again, more insistent. Midas sighed. In the Before Time, Chewy had been programmed with some kind of "adventure encouragement" function—a feature that had probably seemed adorable to children but now registered as passive-aggressive emotional blackmail. Down they went. The basement was, predictably, a trap. Not the kind with spikes—Midas would have preferred spikes. This was the kind where the floor, weakened by decades of water damage, gave way the moment Midas stepped on the wrong tile. He fell eight feet into a sub-basement that shouldn't have existed, landing in something soft and squelching that he chose not to identify. Chewy, still strapped to the pack, clicked reproachfully. "Don't say it." *Click.* Midas's left ankle announced that it was no longer on speaking terms with the rest of his body. His pack had split open, scattering their remaining supplies into the muck. And somewhere above, he could hear the distinctive scrabbling of feral dogs investigating the noise. "Right," he said, through gritted teeth. "New plan." Getting out took four hours, most of Midas's remaining strength, and one truly unfortunate encounter with a territorial raccoon the size of a terrier that had made its nest in the only climbable section of wall. Midas emerged from the basement looking like he'd lost a fight with a compost heap, which, technically, he had. The dogs found them shortly after. Midas ran. His ankle screamed. Chewy bounced against his back with a rhythm of clicks that somehow managed to sound like panicked cursing. They escaped the dogs by climbing a rusted fire escape that groaned under Midas's weight like a dying whale. At the top, he collapsed, gasping, and looked out over the Barrows. No Greenvale. No refuge. Just miles of decay stretching toward a horizon that offered nothing but more of the same. "Well," Midas said, examining his torn jacket, his swollen ankle, and the mysterious brown substance now coating most of his body, "that was a disaster." *Click*, said Chewy, with what Midas could have sworn was satisfaction. The walk home took twice as long as the walk out, owing to the limp, the exhaustion, and one brief but memorable incident involving a wasps' nest. They arrived at the freezer unit just as the sun abandoned them entirely, leaving the world to the grey half-light of the apocalypse's eternal dusk. Midas leaned Chewy against the wall and began the tedious business of not dying—cleaning wounds, rationing what food remained, and wondering, as he always did, whether tomorrow would be any better. "Same time tomorrow?" he asked. *Click*, said Chewy. Midas smiled. It was, after all, the only vote that mattered.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the wry, dry humor characteristic of Terry Pratchett while establishing a compelling post-apocalyptic setting. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt, delivering exactly what was requested: a story beginning with deliberation, featuring a mission gone wrong, and ending with the protagonists worse for wear. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believable protagonist - cynical yet determined, prone to poor decisions but endearingly human in his flaws. The relationship with Chewy is cleverly constructed, using the animatronic's limited "click" responses to create genuine dialogue dynamics. The author skillfully implies Chewy's personality through Midas's interpretations, making the koala feel like a real character despite being an inanimate object. The prose maintains consistent voice throughout, balancing humor with genuine stakes. Lines like "a freezer unit that no longer froze anything except, occasionally, Midas's enthusiasm for living" exemplify the Pratchett-esque wit. The world-building is economical but effective, painting a vivid picture of decay without over-explaining. The plot structure follows the requested arc perfectly - the mission to find Greenvale goes predictably wrong through a series of escalating mishaps. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through the basement trap, dog chase, and various other misfortunes. However, the piece has some weaknesses. While the humor is consistent, some jokes feel slightly forced or predictable. The ending, while appropriate to the tone, lacks emotional depth - the characters seem unchanged by their ordeal. The descriptive passages, while competent, occasionally veer toward the mundane rather than the memorable. The dialogue between Midas and Chewy works well conceptually but becomes somewhat repetitive. The story also relies heavily on familiar post-apocalyptic tropes without adding significant innovation to the genre. Overall, this is a solid, entertaining piece that fulfills its brief competently while maintaining reader engagement throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.9
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: The Bell Above the Door The bell above the door gave its familiar brass tinkle, and Ffion didn't look up from her ledger. "We're closed in twenty—" The door slammed. Locks clicked. She looked up. The man pressing his back against her door was tall, dark-haired, and wearing sunglasses indoors. In Wales. In November. "I'm so sorry," he said, breathless. "There are—there are photographers outside. Like, six of them. I just need—" He pulled the sunglasses off. "Please. Just ten minutes." Ffion recognized him immediately, which annoyed her. Rhys Ashton. Currently starring in whatever medieval knight series had women worldwide clutching their chests. She'd seen his face on the side of a bus in Cardiff last month. He'd been shirtless on the bus. She'd looked at the bus. "There's a back exit through the stockroom," she said, returning to her ledger. "You're welcome to wait, or you're welcome to go." "Wait, that's it? No—" He gestured vaguely at himself, as though presenting a gift. "No 'oh my God, you're Rhys Ashton'?" "Would you like me to say that?" "I—" He paused, recalibrating. "No. Actually, that's... no." He drifted toward the counter, hands in his jacket pockets, radiating an energy that suggested he'd never been told 'no' in his life and was finding it erotically confusing. "This is a great shop. Really atmospheric. How long have you—" "Fourteen years. Since my mother died." She looked up. "I'm not being brusque to be mysterious, by the way. I'm just actually brusque." The corner of his mouth twitched. "That might be the most honest thing anyone's said to me in two years." "Then you need better people." "God, you're not wrong." He leaned against the counter—casual, practiced, his smile warm and surgical. "What's your name?" "Ffion." "Ffion." He said it correctly, which was unusual. "That's beautiful. You must hear that all the time." "You must hear that all the time." His smile faltered, then widened into something less rehearsed. "I walked right into that." "You did." "Usually people just... they give me things, you know? Compliments. Laughs. They laugh at things that aren't even—" He stopped, looking surprised at himself. "I don't know why I'm telling you this." "Because I'm a stranger and you'll never see me again. People confess to booksellers. We're like bartenders, but with better lighting." He laughed—genuinely, his shoulders dropping. "Ffion the bookseller. Unimpressed by fame. Unmoved by—" He gestured at himself again. "Please stop doing that." "Doing what?" "Presenting yourself like a dish at a restaurant." He stared at her, and she watched something shift behind his eyes. The performance was cracking. Good. She was tired of the performance. It was like watching a beautiful mask that occasionally, accidentally, revealed the person underneath. "When I was twelve," he said quietly, "I was in a school play. My father came. Afterward, he said my performance was 'adequate.'" He swallowed. "I've been trying to get a better review ever since." The confession hung between them. Ffion felt her chest tighten. She knew something about inadequate fathers. About trying to build yourself into something they'd have to notice. "That's the saddest thing I've ever heard someone say while smiling," she said. "I'm always smiling. It's a condition." "Must be exhausting." "You have no idea." His voice was barely above a whisper. Then, almost imperceptibly, he leaned closer. Not a move—something involuntary. Like a plant toward light. Ffion's pulse quickened. She hated that it did. She was supposed to be the one unmoved, the steady counterweight to his spinning world. But he was looking at her like she'd said something no one else ever had, and it was doing something dangerous to the wall she'd built. "You have ink on your cheek," he said softly. "Just there." Her hand flew up. "Where?" "Left side. No—may I?" He reached across the counter, his thumb brushing her cheekbone. The touch was featherlight. Barely there. Her breath caught, and she saw his eyes change when it did—saw him register the effect he was having. But for once, he didn't press the advantage. He pulled back, looking almost startled by himself. "Got it," he said hoarsely. Outside, a car door slammed. Then another. Voices drifted past, faded. "Sounds like they've moved on," Ffion said. Her voice was steadier than her heartbeat. "I'll show you the back exit." "Right." He didn't move. "Ffion, I—" "Your ten minutes was up four minutes ago, Mr. Ashton." "Rhys." He stepped back, something raw in his expression. "Please. Call me Rhys." She should show him the exit. She should be brusque, as advertised. She should not, under any circumstances, say what she said next. "The shop opens at nine tomorrow. If you wanted to... browse." His smile returned, but it was different now. Smaller. Real. "I've never been much of a reader." "Then you'd be my greatest project yet." He laughed—soft, wondering. "Tomorrow, then." "Tomorrow." He paused at the stockroom door, turning back. "Ffion? Your father—was he the 'adequate' type too?" She looked at him, this strange man who'd crashed into her quiet life and somehow found the crack in her armor. "He was worse. He left." "I'm sorry." "Don't be. It's why I can spot a performance from thirty yards." His expression flickered—caught between offense and admiration. Admiration won. "Nine o'clock," he said, and disappeared into the dark. Ffion stood very still in the silence he'd left behind. Then she pressed her fingers to her cheekbone, where his thumb had been, and stayed like that for a long time.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the requested romantic tension and character dynamics. The author successfully creates two distinct voices - Rhys's performative charm gradually giving way to vulnerability, and Ffion's dry, defensive wit masking her own susceptibility to attraction. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with each character having a clear voice. Ffion's brusque honesty ("I'm not being brusque to be mysterious, by the way. I'm just actually brusque") contrasts well with Rhys's initial charm offensive. The author skillfully shows the actor's fragile ego through his surprise at not being immediately recognized and his confession about his father's "adequate" review. The Welsh setting is established efficiently without over-description, and the bookstore provides an appropriate intimate space for the encounter. The pacing builds naturally from Rhys's dramatic entrance to the quiet moment of connection when he touches her cheek. However, there are some weaknesses. The transformation from mutual wariness to romantic interest feels somewhat rushed, particularly Ffion's invitation for him to return tomorrow. While her attraction is established through internal narration, the shift from dismissive to inviting could use more gradual development. Some metaphors feel slightly forced ("like a plant toward light"), and occasional phrases veer toward overwrought territory. The ending strikes an appropriate note of possibility without being overly optimistic. The mutual revelation of difficult fathers creates believable common ground, though this parallel feels somewhat convenient. The piece maintains good tension throughout and avoids major clichés, though some romantic beats are familiar. The prose is generally elegant with good rhythm, though it occasionally becomes self-conscious. The author handles the dual perspective well, showing both characters' internal states while maintaining the dialogue-heavy structure requested. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Morska Conspiracy The door yielded to Aya's touch with a groan of ancient hinges, and the smell hit her first—brine and old blood, the permuthane reek of whale oil lamps burning low. The underhold stretched before them, a cathedral of damp stone and brass piping, the vault's iron door visible at the far end like a sleeping leviathan. And standing before it, resplendent in a coat of black oilcloth, was Varen Morska. *Imposs—* The thought died stillborn. The guild mistress stood flanked by two enforcers, their mechanical augmentation hissing steam in the cold air. Clockwork limbs, the left's arm replaced to the elbow with a pneumatic vice. Varen's cutlass hung loose at her hip, its edge catching the lamplight. "Shut the door behind you, darlings," Varen said, her voice carrying the easy authority of a woman who had never once doubted her place in the world. "Wouldn't want the draft." Katra's hand moved toward her blade. The enforcer with the vice-arm took a single clanking step forward, and Katra's hand fell away. "Simeon," Aya whispered. "Don't." The big man's jaw worked, but he held. They were measured, outmatched, three rats in a viper's den. "You're supposed to be in Newgate," Aya said, hating the tremor in her voice. Varen laughed—a sound like coins on a counting house floor. "The Watch? Those tin-soldier fools couldn't hold a fish in a barrel. I walked out the front door an hour after they dragged me in. Commissioner's got more interest in his ledgers than his locks." She tilted her head, studying Aya's masked face with predatory curiosity. "The real question is who among my people thought to test the waters while the cat was away." *She knows. She must know.* "We're not here for trouble," Katra said, her voice steady despite everything. "We're loyal to the guild—" "Loyal." Varen tasted the word. "Loyal souls don't creep into my underhold in the small hours." She made a small gesture. "Remove the mask." Aya's heart hammered against her ribs. The mask—her only shield, the thin porcelain barrier between this life and the ruin of her family name. She had crafted it herself, etched its surface with subtle wards against detection, worn it like a second skin through a hundred midnight errands. "No." The enforcer moved with terrible mechanical efficiency. One moment Aya was standing; the next, cold brass fingers closed around her wrist and wrenched her arm behind her back. She gasped at the sharp bloom of pain. "I won't ask again." A distant foghorn sounded three times in the harbor beyond the guild walls. The long, mournful notes seemed to drain the warmth from the already frigid air. *Stall. You must stall.* Aya reached up with her free hand and lifted the mask away. Varen's expression shifted—first surprise, then something darker, more avaricious. A slow smile spread across her weathered face. "Well, well. The Ashford girl." Varen stepped closer, her boots clicking on the stone. "Lord Ashford's precious daughter, slumming with the thieves. And here I thought you were merely talented. Turns out you're *valuable*." "Let them go," Aya said, hating how small her voice sounded without the mask. "Katra and Simeon—they followed me. They're nothing to you." "They broke into my underhold," Varen said simply. "That's something." "Then take it out on me. I'm worth more alive than dead, and you know it." Varen circled her like a shark scenting chum. "Go on." *Think. Think like Father's daughter. Think like Mother's daughter.* "You drag me before the Commissioner, and what do you get? A pat on the head, maybe. Some coin. But my father—" Aya swallowed. "My father would pay handsomely to keep this quiet. An aristocrat's daughter, running with thieves? The scandal would destroy him. He'd empty his vaults to prevent that." "And if I simply kill you all and dump you in the harbour?" "Then you get nothing but three corpses and a lord's eternal enmity." Aya met her gaze. "I'm worth more as leverage. As insurance. You must know how the great houses operate—they collect debts and favours like you collect coin. My father's gratitude would be... substantial." *And if he refuses to pay? If he lets me hang as Mother did?* The thought came unbidden, cold and sharp. She pushed it away. Varen considered this, her cutlass tapping against her thigh. Behind her, the enforcers stood patient as clockwork, their augmented limbs venting thin streams of steam. "The big one and the girl go to the cells," Varen decided. "You stay with me." Her smile returned, sharper now. "We're going to have a very long conversation about your *other* talents, girl. The ones that don't come from your father's blood." Aya's blood turned to ice. *She knows about the immateria. She knows what I can summon.* "The mask hid more than your face," Varen continued, almost gently. "Did you truly think I wouldn't notice the signs? The cold spots? The way the lamps gutter when you enter a room?" She leaned close, and Aya could smell the whale oil and tobacco on her breath. "Your mother hanged for those same gifts, didn't she? Such a waste. The rope doesn't care about pedigree." Aya said nothing. There was nothing to say. "Take them," Varen commanded, and the enforcers moved. As Katra and Simeon were dragged away into the dark, Aya heard her friend's voice, fierce even in defeat: "We'll find you, Aya. We'll find—" The door closed, cutting off the words. Varen's hand closed around Aya's chin, forcing her to meet those calculating eyes. "Now then," the guild mistress said. "Let's discuss your future."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the complex prompt requirements, successfully incorporating the whalepunk fantasy setting, Victorian gothic atmosphere, and specified story beats. The author effectively establishes the underground setting with sensory details like "brine and old blood, the permuthane reek of whale oil lamps" and mechanical augmentations that reinforce the steampunk aesthetic. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Varen Morska emerges as a compelling antagonist with authentic voice and clear motivations. Her dialogue feels natural and period-appropriate, carrying the authority of someone accustomed to power. Aya's internal struggle between her aristocratic identity and criminal activities creates genuine tension, and her desperation comes through convincingly as she attempts to negotiate for her companions' lives. The prose strikes an effective balance between atmospheric description and narrative momentum. The author avoids purple prose while maintaining rich imagery that serves the gothic tone. Mechanical details like "clockwork limbs" and "pneumatic vice" are integrated naturally without overwhelming the scene. The dialogue feels authentic to both the characters and setting, with Varen's casual menace ("Shut the door behind you, darlings") contrasting effectively with Aya's increasingly desperate attempts at negotiation. The revelation of Aya's magical abilities and family history creates layers of vulnerability that drive the emotional stakes. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the initial shock of discovery through the unmasking to the final separation of the trio. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested while maintaining dramatic tension through psychological confrontation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional reliance on familiar fantasy tropes (the mysterious magical bloodline, the corrupt aristocrat's daughter) though these are executed competently within the established framework. The ending feels appropriately dark and unresolved, fitting the gothic tone without forced positivity.
